plainMeaning: Those same disease-ravaged people, by worshipping your feet, get their health and beauty back — just as mountain trees burnt by a forest fire turn green and lovely once more.
recitation: te tuha calaṇa-ārāhaṇa-salilañjali-seya-vuḍḍhi-ucchāhā, vaṇa-dava-daḍḍhā giri-pāyavā vva pattā puṇo vi lacchiṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: they, those (same afflicted ones of the previous verse)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit te < Skt. te, nom. pl. of tad
wordByWord.notes: Anaphoric — points back to the leprosy-stricken sufferers described in v.2.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tuha < Skt. tava, gen. sg. of tvam
calaṇa (< Skt. caraṇa, foot) + ārāhaṇa (< Skt. ārādhana, worship, propitiation)
wordByWord.notes: First member of the long bahuvrīhi qualifying 'te.'
The act of feet-worship is figured as pouring an añjali of water at the feet.

The pivotal pun: this 'watering' both irrigates the devotee's zeal and answers the rain that revives the scorched trees. A minority reading takes seya as sveda (sweat of effort); seka fits the water/forest-fire imagery better.
Closes the bahuvrīhi: 'te ... ucchāhā' = those whose zeal is swelled by the watering-libation of worshipping your feet.
First member of the simile-compound vaṇa-dava-daḍḍhā.

wordByWord.etymology: dava (< Skt. dava, forest-fire) + daḍḍha (< Skt. dagdha, burnt), nom. pl.
wordByWord.notes: Agrees with giri-pāyavā — 'mountain-trees burnt by forest-fire.'

wordByWord.etymology: giri (mountain) + pāyava (< Skt. pādapa, tree, lit. 'drinking by the foot/root')
wordByWord.notes: The source prints a stray space — 'ગિરિપા યવવ્વ' — so this token and the next together read giri-pāyavā vva. The break is a print/OCR artifact, not a word boundary.

wordByWord.etymology: remainder of pāyavā (pādapāḥ) + vva (< Skt. iva, 'like, as')
wordByWord.notes: vva is the comparative particle 'like'; it joins to the preceding token to give 'like mountain-trees.'

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit patta < Skt. prāpta (past part. of prāp), nom. pl. pattā

wordByWord.notes: puṇo vi = 'once again, even.' Emphasizes that what seemed irrecoverable is in fact recovered.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit lacchī < Skt. lakṣmī; acc. sg. lacchiṁ
wordByWord.notes: Object of pattā. For the trees it is new green foliage; for the devotee it is restored health, beauty and prosperity — the word holds both at once.

literalTranslation: They — their zeal swelled by the watering-libation of the worship of your feet — like mountain-trees scorched by a forest fire, have attained splendour (Lakṣmī) once again.
idiomaticTranslation: Those very sufferers, the fervour of their devotion fed like life-giving water by the worship poured out at your feet, win back their beauty and fortune once more — exactly as the fire-blackened trees on a mountainside burst green and lovely again after the rains.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the second half of the disease-pair, and it turns directly on the horror of v.2. Where v.2 left the body rotting — fingers, nose and beauty consumed by the 'fire-sparks' of leprosy — v.3 answers that fire with water. The whole rescue rides on a single deliberate pun: seya (seka), the 'sprinkling/watering' of the feet-libation, is the same watering that, as rain, revives a scorched forest. So the simile is exact, not decorative: the afflicted are the burnt giri-pāyava (mountain-trees), the fire of leprosy is the dava (wildfire), the worship-water is the rain, and lacchiṁ is the new foliage that returns. Note the grammar: the long compound calaṇa-ārāhaṇa-salilañjali-seya-vuḍḍhi-ucchāhā is a bahuvrīhi describing 'te' — the devotees are defined as 'those whose zeal has grown by that watering.' The cure is therefore not magical substitution but a kindled inner ucchāha (fervour) that worship nourishes. puṇo vi ('even again') carries the emotional weight: what v.2 made to look like permanent ruin is shown to be reversible. lacchiṁ is chosen with care — it means at once foliage, physical beauty, and worldly fortune, so the single word repairs everything the previous verse destroyed.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse embodies the stotra's working premise that smaraṇa-bhakti — devoted remembrance and ārādhana of the Jina's feet — operates as a real, regenerative force in a devotee's life, restoring bodily and worldly well-being. Doctrinally the agent is the bhakta's own cultivated ucchāha (zeal/vīrya): grace is mediated through effortful, watered devotion rather than imposed from outside, consistent with the Jain stress on self-exertion. The pādapa simile also quietly encodes a Jain conviction that catastrophe is impermanent and conditions are reversible through right effort — the burnt tree is not dead but dormant, awaiting its 'rain.'
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a reciter facing illness, disfigurement or ruin, this verse is the deliberate turn from dread to hope: hold the image of the green-again mountainside and let it convert despair into ucchāha — steady, watered devotion — rather than passive waiting. It teaches that recovery is nourished drop by drop, like irrigation, not seized in a single stroke.

crossReferences.text: Preceding gāthā (v.2): the leprosy/great-disease 'fire-sparks' that scorch every limb — the ruin this verse reverses
v.3's nature-simile of the burnt mountain/forest growing green again, flagged in the sutra preface as the recurring 'restoration after ruin' motif
Cf. the parallel deliverance-template in the danger-pairs that follow (sea vv.4–5, fire vv.6–7), and the summary v.18

sources.title: 56 Namiyun Stotra (Romanized) — Jain Quantum
sources.consultedFor: Romanized Prakrit reading of gāthā 3 (te tuha calaṇārāhaṇa … pattā puṇo vi lacchiṁ) and verse sequence

sources.title: Parshvanatha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Pārśva as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara and fear-remover to whom the hymn's deliverance-promise is addressed

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicons (Monier-Williams)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying the Prakrit tokens: seka (sprinkling/watering), vṛddhi, utsāha, lakṣmī, pādapa, dagdha, prāpta

sources.title: H. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit-to-Sanskrit equivalences: vuḍḍhi=vṛddhi, ucchāha=utsāha, lacchī=lakṣmī, calaṇa=caraṇa, vva=iva

notes: Verse text follows the supplied book reading verbatim, including the stray space in 'ગિરિપા યવવ્વ', which is a print/OCR break inside giri-pāyavā vva and not a word boundary. The central interpretive choice is seya = seka ('watering/sprinkling'), which makes the water/forest-fire pun cohere; a minority reading takes seya as sveda ('sweat of devout effort'), shifting the sense to 'zeal swelled by the sweat of feet-worship' — the deliverance is the same either way. lacchiṁ (Lakṣmī) is intentionally polyvalent (foliage / beauty / fortune); rendered to keep all three. Some recensions print the compound and sandhi slightly differently (e.g. vuḍḍhi-acchāyā vs vuḍḍhi-ucchāhā); glosses here follow the supplied Gujarati ઉચ્છાહા = utsāha. The jainqq.org archive page is bot-blocked (HTTP 403) to automated fetches but is a genuine, citable Jain text repository.
